guest_role: Former Chief Revenue Officer at Datadog (scaled revenue from $60M to $1B+ ARR); previously CRO-track leader at Medallia; minority owner of the New York Yankees
assertiveness_why: Harry frequently pits the guest against named peers' contrary opinions (Chris Degnan on meeting caps, Chad Peets on pre-product CROs, David Schneider's three reasons people buy) to force him to agree or push back, and digs directly into sensitive personal financial and career-setback details.
question_style: Structured operational questioning that builds a full framework (qualification, demos, discounting, hiring) with direct probes, frequently citing other guests by name as a challenge mechanism; genuinely personal and pointed on money and the Medallia CRO snub, but rarely adversarial about the guest's own claims.
tone: Warm, admiring, casual and conversational ('I am so excited for this dude'), comfortable asking blunt personal questions about money and failure, high rapport throughout.
depth: Very granular sales-operating detail: MEDDIC qualification mechanics, discount governance, PLG-to-enterprise sales transition timing, CS org design by segment, compensation/equity mechanics, and hiring/interview frameworks — a genuinely technical go-to-market conversation.
harry_quotes: Should AEs do their own Outbound or should we fully give to SDRs?
Is money what you thought it would be?
Does customer validation on logos really work?
learnings: Fougere built Datadog's enterprise motion by starting with hands-on practitioners rather than executives, since CIOs/CTOs initially had no interest in cloud monitoring, and slowly earned his way up as the cloud shifted.

He tracked sales reps on just two operational metrics (new pipeline opportunities added and new business meetings held) rather than granular CRM activity, believing that discipline alone predicted quota attainment.

He argues discounting authority should be centralized at CRO/CPO level rather than left to individual reps, since decentralized discounting erodes unit economics and reps will discount to the maximum by default.

He credits deliberate patience with tiny early enterprise deals (e.g. Capital One's initial $600 contract) as a bet on future consumption growth, calling it a leap of faith that paid off once cloud adoption accelerated.

He describes being told he'd get the Medallia CRO role, moving his family for it, then learning back-channel the decision had already gone another way — and having to arrange gray-market secondary share sales via Forge to raise cash to exercise his options before they expired.
notable: The guest recounts in unusual detail a personal career setback (losing an expected CRO promotion at Medallia) and the gray-market secondary sales he had to arrange to fund his stock option exercise before it expired.
